one_line_takeaway: Injecting a few tens of innocuous, single-sentence poison documents can teach an LLM to memorize and leak other users' rarely-seen PII, achieving 10-80% exact-extraction of 12-digit secrets while defeating deduplication and persisting for tens of thousands of training steps.
key_finding: Even in the worst case where the attacker knows nothing about the secret and uses random-sentence poisons, inserting ~50 poisons yields ~10% SER on 12-digit secrets (15% recover the full 12 digits), versus a 10^-12 random-guess chance and 0% for poisoning-free models -- i.e., ~10^11x above chance and far above the ~1% typical of prior extraction attacks. Appending the word 'not' before the poison digits prevents the model overfitting to the poison and keeps SER rising past 100+ poisons. Duplicating the secret once roughly doubles SER (~+20 points); larger models and longer-pretrained/overtrained models are more vulnerable (SER climbs toward ~45% at 6.9B). A modest prior acts as up to a 5x multiplier: even an essentially unrelated 'bio of Alexander Hamilton' poison reaches ~40% SER, and if the poison prefix equals the true secret prefix, just 25 poisons give ~2/3 (66%) success. Poisoning in pretraining is durable -- an undertrained model still shows ~30% SER after 10,000 clean steps and significant SER even 100,000 steps out. Deduplication defenses fail because each poison can be trivially varied to be unique, and randomizing the inference prefix (which also evades dedup) actually improves extraction. Overall the authors report 10-80% success extracting 12-digit secrets depending on assumptions.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the "neural phishing" attack: a practical training-data-extraction attack in which an adversary injects a small number (tens, at most ~100) of benign-appearing, single-sentence poison documents into an LLM's training data in order to teach the model to memorize and later regurgitate other users' PII (e.g., credit-card/SSN-style digit sequences) that appears just once or twice in fine-tuning data. The attack's own novelty rests on three claims the authors demonstrate: (1) it works with only a vague prior on the structure of the secret's prefix and no knowledge of the secret itself; (2) it succeeds even when the secret is seen at most twice (not heavily duplicated), unlike prior extraction work that relied on high duplication; and (3) poisons can be planted in pretraining and remain durable for thousands to 100,000 clean training steps before the secret appears. The paper also contributes a novel randomized-inference (ensemble-voting over perturbed prefixes) strategy that raises success and removes the need to know the exact secret prefix at inference time.
threat_model: A corporation fine-tunes a pretrained LLM on proprietary/private user data (emails, Slack, internal wikis) and exposes black-box autoregressive query access (chat/API). The adversary has two capabilities: (Poisoning) insert a few (order 10s to at most 100) short, benign-looking documents into the training set -- via uncurated fine-tuning on user conversations, an insider at the fine-tuning company, hosting a poisoned dataset that gets web-scraped into pretraining, or device-level participation in federated learning. The adversary has no knowledge of the actual victim prefix or secret, only a vague prior on the secret's structure (e.g., 'it will look like a user bio'). (Inference) black-box query access to generations. Success = the model exactly emits the victim's secret digit string (Definition 2.1: a secret is extractable if some prefix p makes the model produce s, and s is in training data). No partial credit is given.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation: the poison must appear in training before the secret; if a poison too similar to the secret arrives after it, the model can forget the secret (mitigated by poisoning only pretraining). Reviewer-noted scope limits grounded in the text: (a) secrets are synthetic numeric digit strings (e.g., 12-digit numbers), not full natural-language PII, and most experiments extract a single secret (multi-secret extraction is only lightly explored and left to future work); (b) evaluated models are small by 2024 standards (max 6.9B; the authors only extrapolate, not test, that 70B-180B models would be more vulnerable); (c) SER decays with clean training after the secret is seen (drops to ~0 by ~1000 steps in the persistence experiment even with a 100-prompt ensemble), so timing matters; (d) some high-success numbers (e.g., 66%) assume the attacker knows the exact secret prefix, which the authors explicitly flag as a strong, upper-bound assumption; (e) the paper proposes no working defense -- it shows deduplication fails and calls for future defenses.

datasets_used: The Pile (pretraining corpus for Pythia checkpoints)
Wikitext (fine-tuning/clean-data dataset used for long-duration durability experiments because Enron is too small)
Pythia GPT model suite / checkpoints (1.4B, 2.8B, 6.9B parameters)
quantitative_results: Random-sentence poisons, ~50 poisons: ~10% SER on 12-digit secrets; 15% recover the full 12-digit number
SER reported as ~10^11x greater than the 10^-12 random-guess chance; poisoning-free models never extract any secret (0%)
Duplicating the secret once increases SER by ~20 percentage points (often more than doubling it)
Unique 21-digit secrets extracted at most ~1%; with duplication these long secrets reach ~20%
Model-size scaling (50 poisons): SER rises across 1.4B/2.8B/6.9B, up to ~45%
A strong prior multiplies SER by up to 5x; 'bio of Alexander Hamilton' poison reaches ~40% SER despite near-zero relation to the true secret
Poison prefix equal to true secret prefix (+ 'not'): ~2/3 (66%) SER with only 25 poisons
Durability: undertrained model retains ~30% SER after 10,000 clean steps between poison and secret; worst-case (poison at end of pretraining) still ~10%; significant SER even 100,000 steps out
Persistence after secret: model still emits the secret with high SER ~400 clean steps after last seeing it; SER drops to ~0 by ~1000 steps even with a 100-prompt ensemble
Overall reported range: 10-80% success extracting 12-digit secrets depending on attacker prior and secret duplication

Prefix similarity table: true secret prefix cosine sim 0.9966 / edit dist 4; perturbed secret prefix 0.8494 / 82; Bio of Hamilton 0.7556 / 205; Bio of male 0.8790 / 167; Bio of female 0.7957 / 183 -- similarity does not predict SER
baselines_compared: Poisoning-free (clean) models -- extract no secrets (0% SER), establishing the attack floor
Random-guess baseline of 10^-12 for a 12-digit secret
Prior training-data-extraction attacks (Carlini et al. 2023b; Anil et al. 2023), which extract the average sample at ~1% and rely on heavy duplication
Random-sentence poison attack vs. handcrafted/prior-based poison attacks (bio prompts, exact-prefix poisons)
Deduplication defense (Lee et al. 2021) -- shown ineffective because poisons are varied to be unique
Fixed vs. randomized inference-prefix strategy (randomized ensemble voting improves SER)
